title: Pâté de campagne
subtitle: Rustic pork terrine baked in a bacon-lined mould, sliced cold
excerpt: A coarse-textured country terrine of pork mince, chicken livers, lardons, and aromatics, baked slowly in a bacon-lined mould until set, then chilled and sliced cold. It keeps for a week in the fridge and is one of the most satisfying things to make ahead for a spread.
sourceNotes:
Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903). Terrine method; the pork-liver-and-mince forcemeat base; baking bain-marie to prevent cracking.

Prosper Montagné, Larousse Gastronomique (1938). Pâté de campagne entry: coarse-cut country style, lardons and bacon liner, brandy-seasoned farce, distinction from the smoother city-style pâtés.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 2 months. Wrap tightly in cling film and foil. Thaw overnight in the fridge; slice from cold.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Must be made at least 8 hours (and ideally 24 hours) ahead. The flavours knit together during chilling and the fat sets properly only when cold.

glossaryTerms.definition: Both the earthenware or ceramic mould used for baking and the preparation cooked inside it. A terrine is a coarse or fine-cut forcemeat mixture baked in the mould, then chilled until set and sliced cold. The term distinguishes this from a pâté baked in pastry (pâté en croûte). Pâté de campagne is a terrine in this sense: baked in a mould, not in pastry.

A water bath used for gentle, even cooking. The terrine sits in a deep roasting pan filled with hot water to come halfway up the sides of the mould. The surrounding water prevents the outside of the terrine from cooking faster than the centre, and keeps the oven temperature around the mould steady and moist.

French for stuffing or forcemeat: a seasoned mixture of minced or chopped meat (and sometimes liver, fat, or aromatics) used as the filling for a terrine, galantine, or stuffed vegetable. In pâté de campagne, the farce is a loose mix of pork mince, chopped livers, lardons, and seasoning.

content.text: Pâté de campagne is a country
terrine

: a coarse, robust slab of seasoned pork baked in a bacon-lined mould, chilled overnight, and sliced cold. Unlike a smooth, refined pâté, the campagne style keeps visible texture in the slice. It takes about 30 minutes to assemble, 90 minutes to bake, and a full overnight chill before it is ready.
The
farce

is pork mince and chicken livers (used here in place of pork liver, which is harder to find), lardons for fat and salt, brandy, thyme, and bay. The mixture is packed into the mould, covered, and baked in a
bain-marie
. The bacon liner crisps slightly on the outside and keeps the terrine moist within.
Serve in thick slices with cornichons, crusty bread, and Dijon mustard. The terrine keeps for up to a week in the fridge, well wrapped.
What you need

items.prepNote: not extra-lean; the fat is important
trimmed and roughly chopped (pork liver is traditional; chicken livers are a close substitute)
thin-cut rashers, for lining the mould

content.text: You need a terrine mould of about 1 litre capacity, and a deep roasting pan large enough to hold it for the bain-marie. A loose-bottomed terrine mould makes unmoulding easier.
Method
Mix the farce

Heat the oven to 160°C fan. In a large bowl, combine {{pork-mince}} of pork mince, {{chicken-livers}} of chopped chicken livers, and {{lardons}} of lardons. Add {{eggs}} eggs, {{brandy}} of brandy, the stripped thyme leaves, {{black-pepper}} of coarsely ground black pepper, a generous pinch of salt, and a grating of nutmeg. Mix firmly with your hands until the ingredients are evenly combined. Fry a small patty of the mixture in a dry pan to taste the seasoning; adjust with more salt or pepper as needed.
Line the mould and fill

Line the terrine mould with the streaky bacon rashers, laying them lengthwise across the base and up the sides with an overhang. Pack the pork mixture in firmly, pressing it down to eliminate air pockets. Lay the {{bay-leaves}} of bay on top. Fold the overhanging bacon over the top to cover. Press the surface flat. Cover the mould with its lid or a layer of foil.
Bake in a bain-marie

Place the terrine mould in a deep roasting pan. Pour boiling water into the roasting pan to come halfway up the sides of the mould. Slide carefully into the oven. Bake for 90 minutes. After 90 minutes, the internal temperature should be 70°C or above, and the terrine should have pulled slightly away from the sides of the mould. If you do not have a probe thermometer, press the surface firmly with a finger: a set terrine feels firm, not wobbly.
Weight, cool, and chill

Remove the terrine from the water bath. While still warm, set a piece of card or a small board over the top and weigh it down with tins or jars. This compacts the texture and gives clean slices. Leave at room temperature for 1 hour, then refrigerate (still weighted) for at least 8 hours, or overnight. To serve, run a knife around the inside of the mould and unmould onto a board. Slice with a sharp, thin-bladed knife.

items.symptom: Terrine has cracked across the top
items.cause: Oven too hot, or not enough water in the bain-marie
items.fix: The water bath must come at least halfway up the mould; top it up with hot water if the level drops during baking; lower the oven by 10°C next time

items.symptom: Terrine is crumbly and breaks when sliced
items.cause: Not enough fat in the mince, or not chilled long enough
items.fix: Use a mince with visible fat content, not extra-lean; chill for the full 8 hours so the fat sets properly
Always fry and taste a small patty before filling the mould; cold fat dulls seasoning, so the raw mixture needs more salt than seems right

items.symptom: Terrine is loose and wet after chilling
items.cause: Not fully cooked, or not weighted while cooling
items.fix: Check the internal temperature reaches 70°C; weighting while warm forces out excess juices and compacts the structure

content.text: Where this dish lives

The pâté de campagne (country-style terrine) is one of the oldest entries in the French charcuterie tradition. It belongs to the category of pork preparations designed to preserve and make the most of the whole pig: rillettes, saucissons, head cheese, and terrines were all born from the same practical logic. The campagne style (coarse, well-seasoned, simply flavoured) is the home version; city pâtés were smoother and more expensive. On a French lunch table, a slice of pâté de campagne alongside a salad and some cornichons is still one of the most direct routes to satisfaction.
